steps.title: Sample Proportion vs. Population Proportion
steps.content: Our survey found that 87% of the sample (200 residents) report satisfaction. A sample proportion is an estimate of the true population proportion—not the population value itself. Sampling error means the percentage of all town residents satisfied could differ from 87%.

steps.title: Statement I: Must the Population Be Exactly 87%?
steps.content: Statement I claims that 87% of all town residents are satisfied. This would only be true if our sample proportion exactly matched the population proportion. However, due to sampling error, the true population proportion could be 85%, 88%, 89%, or another nearby value. Statement I is not necessarily true.

steps.title: Statement II: Will the Next Sample Also Show 87%?
steps.content: Statement II claims that another random sample of 200 residents would again show 87% satisfied. This overlooks sampling variability—the natural variation between different random samples. Different samples from the same population yield different results: one sample shows 87%, another 84%, another 86%. There is no reason to expect the second sample to match the first. Statement II is not necessarily true.

steps.title: Conclusion
steps.content: Since neither Statement I nor Statement II must be true, we eliminate choices B (I only), C (II only), and D (I and II). The answer is choice A — A is correct.
